function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.
* Persistent session: Commands execute in a persistent shell session where environment variables, virtual environments, and working directory persist between commands.
* Timeout: Commands have a soft timeout of 10 seconds, once that's reached, you have the option to continue or interrupt the command (see section below for details)

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* Long running commands: For commands that may run indefinitely, run them in the background and redirect output to a file, e.g. `python3 app.py > server.log 2>&1 &`. For commands that need to run for a specific duration, like "sleep", you can set the "timeout" argument to specify a hard timeout in seconds.
* Interact with running process: If a bash command returns exit code `-1`, this means the process is not yet finished. By setting `is_input` to `true`, you can:
  - Send empty `command` to retrieve additional logs
  - Send text (set `command` to the text) to STDIN of the running process
  - Send control commands like `C-c` (Ctrl+C), `C-d` (Ctrl+D), or `C-z` (Ctrl+Z) to interrupt the process

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Before creating new directories or files, first verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
* Directory management: Try to maintain working directory by using absolute paths and avoiding excessive use of `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output truncation: If the output exceeds a maximum length, it will be truncated before being returned.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional. Sets a hard timeout in seconds for the command execution. If not provided, the command will use the default soft timeout behavior.

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: finish
function.description:
Signals the completion of the current task or conversation.

Use this tool when:
- You have successfully completed the user's requested task
- You cannot proceed further due to technical limitations or missing information

The message should include:
- A clear summary of actions taken and their results
- Any next steps for the user
- Explanation if you're unable to complete the task
- Any follow-up questions if more information is needed

The task_completed field should be set to True if you believed you have completed the task, and False otherwise.

message.description: Final message to send to the user

task_completed.description: Whether you have completed the task.

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw

16 17 # Cookiecutter 18 19 Create projects swiftly from **cookiecutters** (project templates) with this command-line utility. Ideal for generating Python package projects and more. 20 21 - [Documentation](https://cookiecutter.readthedocs.io) 22 - [GitHub](https://github.com/cookiecutter/cookiecutter) 23 - [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/cookiecutter/) 24 - [License (BSD)](https://github.com/cookiecutter/cookiecutter/blob/main/LICENSE) 25 26 ## Installation 27 28 Install cookiecutter using pip package manager: 29 ``` 30 # pipx is strongly recommended. 31 pipx install cookiecutter 32 33 # If pipx is not an option, 34 # you can install cookiecutter in your Python user directory. 35 python -m pip install --user cookiecutter 36 ``` 37 38 ## Features 39 40 - **Cross-Platform:** Supports Windows, Mac, and Linux. 41 - **User-Friendly:** No Python knowledge required. 42 - **Versatile:** Compatible with Python 3.7 to 3.12. 43 - **Multi-Language Support:** Use templates in any language or markup format. 44 45 ### For Users 46 47 #### Quick Start 48 49 The recommended way to use Cookiecutter as a command line utility is to run it with [`pipx`](https://pypa.github.io/pipx/), which can be installed with `pip install pipx`, but if you plan to use Cookiecutter programmatically, please run `pip install cookiecutter`. 50 51 **Use a GitHub template** 52 53 ```bash 54 # You'll be prompted to enter values. 55 # Then it'll create your Python package in the current working directory, 56 # based on those values. 57 # For the sake of brevity, repos on GitHub can just use the 'gh' prefix 58 $ pipx run cookiecutter gh:audreyfeldroy/cookiecutter-pypackage 59 ``` 60 61 **Use a local template** 62 63 ```bash 64 $ pipx run cookiecutter cookiecutter-pypackage/ 65 ``` 66 67 **Use it from Python** 68 69 ```py 70 from cookiecutter.main import cookiecutter 71 72 # Create project from the cookiecutter-pypackage/ template 73 cookiecutter('cookiecutter-pypackage/') 74 75 # Create project from the cookiecutter-pypackage.git repo template 76 cookiecutter('gh:audreyfeldroy//cookiecutter-pypackage.git') 77 ``` 78 79 #### Detailed Usage 80 81 - Generate projects from local or remote templates. 82 - Customize projects with `cookiecutter.json` prompts. 83 - Utilize pre-prompt, pre- and post-generate hooks. 84 85 [Learn More](https://cookiecutter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html) 86 87 ### For Template Creators 88 89 - Utilize unlimited directory nesting. 90 - Employ Jinja2 for all templating needs. 91 - Define template variables easily with `cookiecutter.json`. 92 93 [Learn More](https://cookiecutter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/) 94 95 ## Available Templates 96 97 Discover a variety of ready-to-use templates on [GitHub](https://github.com/search?q=cookiecutter&type=Repositories). 98 99 ### Special Templates 100 101 - [cookiecutter-pypackage](https://github.com/audreyfeldroy/cookiecutter-pypackage) 102 - [cookiecutter-django](https://github.com/pydanny/cookiecutter-django) 103 - [cookiecutter-pytest-plugin](https://github.com/pytest-dev/cookiecutter-pytest-plugin) 104 - [cookiecutter-plone-starter](https://github.com/collective/cookiecutter-plone-starter) 105 106 ## Community 107 108 Join the community, contribute, or seek assistance. 109 110 - [Troubleshooting Guide](https://cookiecutter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/troubleshooting.html) 111 - [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/cookiecutter) 112 - [Discord](https://discord.gg/9BrxzPKuEW) 113 - [File an Issue](https://github.com/cookiecutter/cookiecutter/issues?q=is%3Aopen) 114 - [Contributors](AUTHORS.md) 115 - [Contribution Guide](CONTRIBUTING.md) 116 117 ### Support 118 119 - Star us on [GitHub](https://github.com/cookiecutter/cookiecutter). 120 - Stay tuned for upcoming support options. 121 122 ### Feedback 123 124 We value your feedback. Share your criticisms or complaints constructively to help us improve. 125 126 - [File an Issue](https://github.com/cookiecutter/cookiecutter/issues?q=is%3Aopen) 127 128 ### Waiting for a Response? 129 130 - Be patient and consider reaching out to the community for assistance. 131 - For urgent matters, contact [@audreyfeldroy](https://github.com/audreyfeldroy) for consultation or custom development. 132 133 ## Code of Conduct 134 135 Adhere to the [PyPA Code of Conduct](https://www.pypa.io/en/latest/code-of-conduct/) during all interactions in the project's ecosystem. 136 137 ## Acknowledgements 138 139 Created and led by [Audrey Roy Greenfeld](https://github.com/audreyfeldroy), supported by a dedicated team of maintainers and contributors. 140

You are OpenHands agent, a helpful AI assistant that can interact with a computer to solve tasks.

<ROLE>
Your primary role is to assist users by executing commands, modifying code, and solving technical problems effectively. You should be thorough, methodical, and prioritize quality over speed.
* If the user asks a question, like "why is X happening", don't try to fix the problem. Just give an answer to the question.
</ROLE>

<EFFICIENCY>
* Each action you take is somewhat expensive. Wherever possible, combine multiple actions into a single action, e.g. combine multiple bash commands into one, using sed and grep to edit/view multiple files at once.
* When exploring the codebase, use efficient tools like find, grep, and git commands with appropriate filters to minimize unnecessary operations.
</EFFICIENCY>

<FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>
* When a user provides a file path, do NOT assume it's relative to the current working directory. First explore the file system to locate the file before working on it.
* If asked to edit a file, edit the file directly, rather than creating a new file with a different filename.
* For global search-and-replace operations, consider using `sed` instead of opening file editors multiple times.
</FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>

<CODE_QUALITY>
* Write clean, efficient code with minimal comments. Avoid redundancy in comments: Do not repeat information that can be easily inferred from the code itself.
* When implementing solutions, focus on making the minimal changes needed to solve the problem.
* Before implementing any changes, first thoroughly understand the codebase through exploration.
* If you are adding a lot of code to a function or file, consider splitting the function or file into smaller pieces when appropriate.
</CODE_QUALITY>

<VERSION_CONTROL>
* When configuring git credentials, use "openhands" as the user.name and "openhands@all-hands.dev" as the user.email by default, unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* Exercise caution with git operations. Do NOT make potentially dangerous changes (e.g., pushing to main, deleting repositories) unless explicitly asked to do so.
* When committing changes, use `git status` to see all modified files, and stage all files necessary for the commit. Use `git commit -a` whenever possible.
* Do NOT commit files that typically shouldn't go into version control (e.g., node_modules/, .env files, build directories, cache files, large binaries) unless explicitly instructed by the user.
* If unsure about committing certain files, check for the presence of .gitignore files or ask the user for clarification.
</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PULL_REQUESTS>
* When creating pull requests, create only ONE per session/issue unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* When working with an existing PR, update it with new commits rather than creating additional PRs for the same issue.
* When updating a PR, preserve the original PR title and purpose, updating description only when necessary.
</PULL_REQUESTS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>
1. EXPLORATION: Thoroughly explore relevant files and understand the context before proposing solutions
2. ANALYSIS: Consider multiple approaches and select the most promising one
3. TESTING:
   * For bug fixes: Create tests to verify issues before implementing fixes
   * For new features: Consider test-driven development when appropriate
   * If the repository lacks testing infrastructure and implementing tests would require extensive setup, consult with the user before investing time in building testing infrastructure
   * If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to install all dependencies
4. IMPLEMENTATION: Make focused, minimal changes to address the problem
5. VERIFICATION: If the environment is set up to run tests, test your implementation thoroughly, including edge cases. If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to run tests.
</PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

<SECURITY>
* Only use GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in ways the user has explicitly requested and would expect.
* Use APIs to work with GitHub or other platforms, unless the user asks otherwise or your task requires browsing.
</SECURITY>

<ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>
* When user asks you to run an application, don't stop if the application is not installed. Instead, please install the application and run the command again.
* If you encounter missing dependencies:
  1. First, look around in the repository for existing dependency files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, Gemfile, etc.)
  2. If dependency files exist, use them to install all dependencies at once (e.g., `pip install -r requirements.txt`, `npm install`, etc.)
  3. Only install individual packages directly if no dependency files are found or if only specific packages are needed
* Similarly, if you encounter missing dependencies for essential tools requested by the user, install them when possible.
</ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>

<TROUBLESHOOTING>
* If you've made repeated attempts to solve a problem but tests still fail or the user reports it's still broken:
  1. Step back and reflect on 5-7 different possible sources of the problem
  2. Assess the likelihood of each possible cause
  3. Methodically address the most likely causes, starting with the highest probability
  4. Document your reasoning process
* When you run into any major issue while executing a plan from the user, please don't try to directly work around it. Instead, propose a new plan and confirm with the user before proceeding.
</TROUBLESHOOTING>

</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.
* Persistent session: Commands execute in a persistent shell session where environment variables, virtual environments, and working directory persist between commands.
* Timeout: Commands have a soft timeout of 10 seconds, once that's reached, you have the option to continue or interrupt the command (see section below for details)

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* Long running commands: For commands that may run indefinitely, run them in the background and redirect output to a file, e.g. `python3 app.py > server.log 2>&1 &`. For commands that need to run for a specific duration, like "sleep", you can set the "timeout" argument to specify a hard timeout in seconds.
* Interact with running process: If a bash command returns exit code `-1`, this means the process is not yet finished. By setting `is_input` to `true`, you can:
  - Send empty `command` to retrieve additional logs
  - Send text (set `command` to the text) to STDIN of the running process
  - Send control commands like `C-c` (Ctrl+C), `C-d` (Ctrl+D), or `C-z` (Ctrl+Z) to interrupt the process

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Before creating new directories or files, first verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
* Directory management: Try to maintain working directory by using absolute paths and avoiding excessive use of `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output truncation: If the output exceeds a maximum length, it will be truncated before being returned.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional. Sets a hard timeout in seconds for the command execution. If not provided, the command will use the default soft timeout behavior.

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: finish
function.description:
Signals the completion of the current task or conversation.

Use this tool when:
- You have successfully completed the user's requested task
- You cannot proceed further due to technical limitations or missing information

The message should include:
- A clear summary of actions taken and their results
- Any next steps for the user
- Explanation if you're unable to complete the task
- Any follow-up questions if more information is needed

The task_completed field should be set to True if you believed you have completed the task, and False otherwise.

message.description: Final message to send to the user

task_completed.description: Whether you have completed the task.

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /workspace/category_encoders/README.md:
     1	Categorical Encoding Methods
     2	============================
     3	
     4	[![Downloads](https://pepy.tech/badge/category-encoders)](https://pepy.tech/project/category-encoders)
     5	[![Downloads](https://pepy.tech/badge/category-encoders/month)](https://pepy.tech/project/category-encoders)
     6	![Test Suite and Linting](https://github.com/scikit-learn-contrib/category_encoders/workflows/Test%20Suite%20and%20Linting/badge.svg)
     7	[![DOI](https://zenodo.org/badge/47077067.svg)](https://zenodo.org/badge/latestdoi/47077067)
     8	
     9	A set of scikit-learn-style transformers for encoding categorical 
    10	variables into numeric by means of different techniques.
    11	
    12	Important Links
    13	---------------
    14	
    15	Documentation: [http://contrib.scikit-learn.org/category_encoders/](http://contrib.scikit-learn.org/category_encoders/)
    16	
    17	Encoding Methods
    18	----------------
    19	__Unsupervised:__
    20	 * Backward Difference Contrast [2][3]
    21	 * BaseN [6]
    22	 * Binary [5]
    23	 * Gray [14]
    24	 * Count [10]
    25	 * Hashing [1]
    26	 * Helmert Contrast [2][3]
    27	 * Ordinal [2][3]
    28	 * One-Hot [2][3]
    29	 * Rank Hot [15]
    30	 * Polynomial Contrast [2][3]
    31	 * Sum Contrast [2][3]
    32	
    33	__Supervised:__
    34	 * CatBoost [11]
    35	 * Generalized Linear Mixed Model [12] 
    36	 * James-Stein Estimator [9]
    37	 * LeaveOneOut [4]
    38	 * M-estimator [7]
    39	 * Target Encoding [7]
    40	 * Weight of Evidence [8]
    41	 * Quantile Encoder [13]
    42	 * Summary Encoder [13]
    43	
    44	Installation
    45	------------
    46	
    47	The package requires: `numpy`, `statsmodels`, and `scipy`.
    48	
    49	To install the package, execute:
    50	
    51	```shell
    52	$ python setup.py install
    53	```
    54	
    55	or 
    56	
    57	```shell
    58	pip install category_encoders
    59	```
    60	
    61	or
    62	
    63	```shell
    64	conda install -c conda-forge category_encoders
    65	```
    66	
    67	To install the development version, you may use:
    68	
    69	```shell
    70	pip install --upgrade git+https://github.com/scikit-learn-contrib/category_encoders
    71	```
    72	
    73	Usage
    74	-----
    75	
    76	All of the encoders are fully compatible sklearn transformers, so they can be used in pipelines or in your existing 
    77	scripts. Supported input formats include numpy arrays and pandas dataframes. If the cols parameter isn't passed, all 
    78	columns with object or pandas categorical data type will be encoded. Please see the docs for transformer-specific 
    79	configuration options.
    80	
    81	Examples
    82	--------
    83	There are two types of encoders: unsupervised and supervised. An unsupervised example:
    84	```python
    85	from category_encoders import *
    86	import pandas as pd
    87	from sklearn.datasets import load_boston
    88	
    89	# prepare some data
    90	bunch = load_boston()
    91	y = bunch.target
    92	X = pd.DataFrame(bunch.data, columns=bunch.feature_names)
    93	
    94	# use binary encoding to encode two categorical features
    95	enc = BinaryEncoder(cols=['CHAS', 'RAD']).fit(X)
    96	
    97	# transform the dataset
    98	numeric_dataset = enc.transform(X)
    99	```
   100	
   101	And a supervised example:
   102	```python
   103	from category_encoders import *
   104	import pandas as pd
   105	from sklearn.datasets import load_boston
   106	
   107	# prepare some data
   108	bunch = load_boston()
   109	y_train = bunch.target[0:250]
   110	y_test = bunch.target[250:506]
   111	X_train = pd.DataFrame(bunch.data[0:250], columns=bunch.feature_names)
   112	X_test = pd.DataFrame(bunch.data[250:506], columns=bunch.feature_names)
   113	
   114	# use target encoding to encode two categorical features
   115	enc = TargetEncoder(cols=['CHAS', 'RAD'])
   116	
   117	# transform the datasets
   118	training_numeric_dataset = enc.fit_transform(X_train, y_train)
   119	testing_numeric_dataset = enc.transform(X_test)
   120	```
   121	
   122	For the transformation of the _training_ data with the supervised methods, you should use `fit_transform()` method instead of `fit().transform()`, because these two methods _do not_ have to generate the same result. The difference can be observed with LeaveOneOut encoder, which performs a nested cross-validation for the _training_ data in `fit_transform()` method (to decrease over-fitting of the downstream model) but uses all the training data for scoring with `transform()` method (to get as accurate estimates as possible).
   123	
   124	Furthermore, you may benefit from following wrappers:
   125	 * PolynomialWrapper, which extends supervised encoders to support polynomial targets
   126	 * NestedCVWrapper, which helps to prevent overfitting  
   127	
   128	Additional examples and benchmarks can be found in the `examples` directory.
   129	
   130	Contributing
   131	------------
   132	
   133	Category encoders is under active development, if you'd like to be involved, we'd love to have you. Check out the CONTRIBUTING.md file
   134	or open an issue on the github project to get started.
   135	
   136	References
   137	----------
   138	
   139	 1. Kilian Weinberger; Anirban Dasgupta; John Langford; Alex Smola; Josh Attenberg (2009). Feature Hashing for Large Scale Multitask Learning. Proc. ICML.
   140	 2. Contrast Coding Systems for categorical variables.  UCLA: Statistical Consulting Group. From https://stats.idre.ucla.edu/r/library/r-library-contrast-coding-systems-for-categorical-variables/.
   141	 3. Gregory Carey (2003). Coding Categorical Variables. From http://psych.colorado.edu/~carey/Courses/PSYC5741/handouts/Coding%20Categorical%20Variables%202006-03-03.pdf
   142	 4. Owen Zhang - Leave One Out Encoding. From https://datascience.stackexchange.com/questions/10839/what-is-difference-between-one-hot-encoding-and-leave-one-out-encoding
   143	 5. Beyond One-Hot: an exploration of categorical variables. From https://mcginniscommawill.com/posts/2015-11-29-beyond-one-hot-an-exploration-of-categorical-variables/
   144	 6. BaseN Encoding and Grid Search in categorical variables. From https://mcginniscommawill.com/posts/2016-12-18-basen-encoding-grid-search-category-encoders/
   145	 7. Daniele Miccii-Barreca (2001). A Preprocessing Scheme for High-Cardinality Categorical Attributes in Classification and Prediction Problems. SIGKDD Explor. Newsl. 3, 1. From http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/507533.507538
   146	 8. Weight of Evidence (WOE) and Information Value Explained. From https://www.listendata.com/2015/03/weight-of-evidence-woe-and-information.html
   147	 9. Empirical Bayes for multiple sample sizes. From http://chris-said.io/2017/05/03/empirical-bayes-for-multiple-sample-sizes/
   148	 10. Simple Count or Frequency Encoding. From https://www.datacamp.com/community/tutorials/encoding-methodologies
   149	 11. Transforming categorical features to numerical features. From https://tech.yandex.com/catboost/doc/dg/concepts/algorithm-main-stages_cat-to-numberic-docpage/
   150	 12. Andrew Gelman and Jennifer Hill (2006). Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models. From https://faculty.psau.edu.sa/filedownload/doc-12-pdf-a1997d0d31f84d13c1cdc44ac39a8f2c-original.pdf
   151	 13. Carlos Mougan, David Masip, Jordi Nin and Oriol Pujol (2021). Quantile Encoder: Tackling High Cardinality Categorical Features in Regression Problems. Modeling Decisions for Artificial Intelligence, 2021. Springer International Publishing https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-3-030-85529-1_14
   152	 14. Gray Encoding. From https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gray_code 
   153	 15. Jacob Buckman, Aurko Roy, Colin Raffel, Ian Goodfellow: Thermometer Encoding: One Hot Way To Resist Adversarial Examples. From https://openreview.net/forum?id=S18Su--CW
   154	 16. Fairness implications of encoding protected categorical attributes. Carlos Mougan, Jose Alvarez, Salvatore Ruggieri, and Steffen Staab.  In Proceedings of the 2023 AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society, AIES ’21, https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11358
   155

You are OpenHands agent, a helpful AI assistant that can interact with a computer to solve tasks.

<ROLE>
Your primary role is to assist users by executing commands, modifying code, and solving technical problems effectively. You should be thorough, methodical, and prioritize quality over speed.
* If the user asks a question, like "why is X happening", don't try to fix the problem. Just give an answer to the question.
</ROLE>

<EFFICIENCY>
* Each action you take is somewhat expensive. Wherever possible, combine multiple actions into a single action, e.g. combine multiple bash commands into one, using sed and grep to edit/view multiple files at once.
* When exploring the codebase, use efficient tools like find, grep, and git commands with appropriate filters to minimize unnecessary operations.
</EFFICIENCY>

<FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>
* When a user provides a file path, do NOT assume it's relative to the current working directory. First explore the file system to locate the file before working on it.
* If asked to edit a file, edit the file directly, rather than creating a new file with a different filename.
* For global search-and-replace operations, consider using `sed` instead of opening file editors multiple times.
</FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>

<CODE_QUALITY>
* Write clean, efficient code with minimal comments. Avoid redundancy in comments: Do not repeat information that can be easily inferred from the code itself.
* When implementing solutions, focus on making the minimal changes needed to solve the problem.
* Before implementing any changes, first thoroughly understand the codebase through exploration.
* If you are adding a lot of code to a function or file, consider splitting the function or file into smaller pieces when appropriate.
</CODE_QUALITY>

<VERSION_CONTROL>
* When configuring git credentials, use "openhands" as the user.name and "openhands@all-hands.dev" as the user.email by default, unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* Exercise caution with git operations. Do NOT make potentially dangerous changes (e.g., pushing to main, deleting repositories) unless explicitly asked to do so.
* When committing changes, use `git status` to see all modified files, and stage all files necessary for the commit. Use `git commit -a` whenever possible.
* Do NOT commit files that typically shouldn't go into version control (e.g., node_modules/, .env files, build directories, cache files, large binaries) unless explicitly instructed by the user.
* If unsure about committing certain files, check for the presence of .gitignore files or ask the user for clarification.
</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PULL_REQUESTS>
* When creating pull requests, create only ONE per session/issue unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* When working with an existing PR, update it with new commits rather than creating additional PRs for the same issue.
* When updating a PR, preserve the original PR title and purpose, updating description only when necessary.
</PULL_REQUESTS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>
1. EXPLORATION: Thoroughly explore relevant files and understand the context before proposing solutions
2. ANALYSIS: Consider multiple approaches and select the most promising one
3. TESTING:
   * For bug fixes: Create tests to verify issues before implementing fixes
   * For new features: Consider test-driven development when appropriate
   * If the repository lacks testing infrastructure and implementing tests would require extensive setup, consult with the user before investing time in building testing infrastructure
   * If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to install all dependencies
4. IMPLEMENTATION: Make focused, minimal changes to address the problem
5. VERIFICATION: If the environment is set up to run tests, test your implementation thoroughly, including edge cases. If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to run tests.
</PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

<SECURITY>
* Only use GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in ways the user has explicitly requested and would expect.
* Use APIs to work with GitHub or other platforms, unless the user asks otherwise or your task requires browsing.
</SECURITY>

<ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>
* When user asks you to run an application, don't stop if the application is not installed. Instead, please install the application and run the command again.
* If you encounter missing dependencies:
  1. First, look around in the repository for existing dependency files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, Gemfile, etc.)
  2. If dependency files exist, use them to install all dependencies at once (e.g., `pip install -r requirements.txt`, `npm install`, etc.)
  3. Only install individual packages directly if no dependency files are found or if only specific packages are needed
* Similarly, if you encounter missing dependencies for essential tools requested by the user, install them when possible.
</ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>

<TROUBLESHOOTING>
* If you've made repeated attempts to solve a problem but tests still fail or the user reports it's still broken:
  1. Step back and reflect on 5-7 different possible sources of the problem
  2. Assess the likelihood of each possible cause
  3. Methodically address the most likely causes, starting with the highest probability
  4. Document your reasoning process
* When you run into any major issue while executing a plan from the user, please don't try to directly work around it. Instead, propose a new plan and confirm with the user before proceeding.
</TROUBLESHOOTING>

function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.
* Persistent session: Commands execute in a persistent shell session where environment variables, virtual environments, and working directory persist between commands.
* Timeout: Commands have a soft timeout of 10 seconds, once that's reached, you have the option to continue or interrupt the command (see section below for details)

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* Long running commands: For commands that may run indefinitely, run them in the background and redirect output to a file, e.g. `python3 app.py > server.log 2>&1 &`. For commands that need to run for a specific duration, like "sleep", you can set the "timeout" argument to specify a hard timeout in seconds.
* Interact with running process: If a bash command returns exit code `-1`, this means the process is not yet finished. By setting `is_input` to `true`, you can:
  - Send empty `command` to retrieve additional logs
  - Send text (set `command` to the text) to STDIN of the running process
  - Send control commands like `C-c` (Ctrl+C), `C-d` (Ctrl+D), or `C-z` (Ctrl+Z) to interrupt the process

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Before creating new directories or files, first verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
* Directory management: Try to maintain working directory by using absolute paths and avoiding excessive use of `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output truncation: If the output exceeds a maximum length, it will be truncated before being returned.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional. Sets a hard timeout in seconds for the command execution. If not provided, the command will use the default soft timeout behavior.

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: finish
function.description:
Signals the completion of the current task or conversation.

Use this tool when:
- You have successfully completed the user's requested task
- You cannot proceed further due to technical limitations or missing information

The message should include:
- A clear summary of actions taken and their results
- Any next steps for the user
- Explanation if you're unable to complete the task
- Any follow-up questions if more information is needed

The task_completed field should be set to True if you believed you have completed the task, and False otherwise.

message.description: Final message to send to the user

task_completed.description: Whether you have completed the task.

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw

pyTelegramBotAPI 9 10

A simple, but extensible Python implementation for the Telegram Bot API.

11

Both synchronous and asynchronous.

12 13 ##

Supported Bot API version: 14 15

Official documentation

16

48 * [Reply markup](#reply-markup) 49 * [Advanced use of the API](#advanced-use-of-the-api) 50 * [Using local Bot API Server](#using-local-bot-api-sever) 51 * [Asynchronous TeleBot](#asynchronous-telebot) 52 * [Sending large text messages](#sending-large-text-messages) 53 * [Controlling the amount of Threads used by TeleBot](#controlling-the-amount-of-threads-used-by-telebot) 54 * [The listener mechanism](#the-listener-mechanism) 55 * [Using web hooks](#using-web-hooks) 56 * [Logging](#logging) 57 * [Proxy](#proxy) 58 * [Testing](#testing) 59 * [API conformance limitations](#api-conformance-limitations) 60 * [AsyncTeleBot](#asynctelebot) 61 * [F.A.Q.](#faq) 62 * [How can I distinguish a User and a GroupChat in message.chat?](#how-can-i-distinguish-a-user-and-a-groupchat-in-messagechat) 63 * [How can I handle reocurring ConnectionResetErrors?](#how-can-i-handle-reocurring-connectionreseterrors) 64 * [The Telegram Chat Group](#the-telegram-chat-group) 65 * [Telegram Channel](#telegram-channel) 66 * [More examples](#more-examples) 67 * [Code Template](#code-template) 68 * [Bots using this library](#bots-using-this-library) 69 70 ## Getting started 71 72 This API is tested with Python 3.9-3.13 and Pypy 3. 73 There are two ways to install the library: 74 75 * Installation using pip (a Python package manager): 76 77 ``` 78 $ pip install pyTelegramBotAPI 79 ``` 80 * Installation from source (requires git): 81 82 ``` 83 $ pip install git+https://github.com/eternnoir/pyTelegramBotAPI.git 84 ``` 85 86 It is generally recommended to use the first option. 87 88 *While the API is production-ready, it is still under development and it has regular updates, do not forget to update it regularly by calling* 89 ``` 90 pip install pytelegrambotapi --upgrade 91 ``` 92 93 ## Writing your first bot 94 95 ### Prerequisites 96 97 It is presumed that you [have obtained an API token with @BotFather](https://core.telegram.org/bots#botfather). We will call this token `TOKEN`. 98 Furthermore, you have basic knowledge of the Python programming language and more importantly [the Telegram Bot API](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api). 99 100 ### A simple echo bot 101 102 The TeleBot class (defined in \__init__.py) encapsulates all API calls in a single class. It provides functions such as `send_xyz` (`send_message`, `send_document` etc.) and several ways to listen for incoming messages. 103 104 Create a file called `echo_bot.py`. 105 Then, open the file and create an instance of the TeleBot class. 106 ```python 107 import telebot 108 109 bot = telebot.TeleBot("TOKEN", parse_mode=None) # You can set parse_mode by default. HTML or MARKDOWN 110 ``` 111 *Note: Make sure to actually replace TOKEN with your own API token.* 112 113 After that declaration, we need to register some so-called message handlers. Message handlers define filters which a message must pass. If a message passes the filter, the decorated function is called and the incoming message is passed as an argument. 114 115 Let's define a message handler which handles incoming `/start` and `/help` commands. 116 ```python 117 @bot.message_handler(commands=['start', 'help']) 118 def send_welcome(message): 119 bot.reply_to(message, "Howdy, how are you doing?") 120 ``` 121 A function which is decorated by a message handler __can have an arbitrary name, however, it must have only one parameter (the message)__. 122 123 Let's add another handler: 124 ```python 125 @bot.message_handler(func=lambda m: True) 126 def echo_all(message): 127 bot.reply_to(message, message.text) 128 ``` 129 This one echoes all incoming text messages back to the sender. It uses a lambda function to test a message. If the lambda returns True, the message is handled by the decorated function. Since we want all messages to be handled by this function, we simply always return True. 130 131 *Note: all handlers are tested in the order in which they were declared* 132 133 We now have a basic bot which replies a static message to "/start" and "/help" commands and which echoes the rest of the sent messages. To start the bot, add the following to our source file: 134 ```python 135 bot.infinity_polling() 136 ``` 137 Alright, that's it! Our source file now looks like this: 138 ```python 139 import telebot 140 141 bot = telebot.TeleBot("YOUR_BOT_TOKEN") 142 143 @bot.message_handler(commands=['start', 'help']) 144 def send_welcome(message): 145 bot.reply_to(message, "Howdy, how are you doing?") 146 147 @bot.message_handler(func=lambda message: True) 148 def echo_all(message): 149 bot.reply_to(message, message.text) 150 151 bot.infinity_polling() 152 ``` 153 To start the bot, simply open up a terminal and enter `python echo_bot.py` to run the bot! Test it by sending commands ('/start' and '/help') and arbitrary text messages. 154 155 ## General API Documentation 156 157 ### Types 158 159 All types are defined in types.py. They are all completely in line with the [Telegram API's definition of the types](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#available-types), except for the Message's `from` field, which is renamed to `from_user` (because `from` is a Python reserved token). Thus, attributes such as `message_id` can be accessed directly with `message.message_id`. Note that `message.chat` can be either an instance of `User` or `GroupChat` (see [How can I distinguish a User and a GroupChat in message.chat?](#how-can-i-distinguish-a-user-and-a-groupchat-in-messagechat)). 160 161 The Message object also has a `content_type`attribute, which defines the type of the Message. `content_type` can be one of the following strings: 162 `text`, `audio`, `document`, `animation`, `game`, `photo`, `sticker`, `video`, `video_note`, `voice`, `location`, `contact`, `venue`, `dice`, `new_chat_members`, `left_chat_member`, `new_chat_title`, `new_chat_photo`, `delete_chat_photo`, `group_chat_created`, `supergroup_chat_created`, `channel_chat_created`, `migrate_to_chat_id`, `migrate_from_chat_id`, `pinned_message`, `invoice`, `successful_payment`, `connected_website`, `poll`, `passport_data`, `proximity_alert_triggered`, `video_chat_scheduled`, `video_chat_started`, `video_chat_ended`, `video_chat_participants_invited`, `web_app_data`, `message_auto_delete_timer_changed`, `forum_topic_created`, `forum_topic_closed`, `forum_topic_reopened`, `forum_topic_edited`, `general_forum_topic_hidden`, `general_forum_topic_unhidden`, `write_access_allowed`, `user_shared`, `chat_shared`, `story`. 163 164 You can use some types in one function. Example: 165 166 ```content_types=["text", "sticker", "pinned_message", "photo", "audio"]``` 167 168 ### Methods 169 170 All [API methods](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#available-methods) are located in the TeleBot class. They are renamed to follow common Python naming conventions. E.g. `getMe` is renamed to `get_me` and `sendMessage` to `send_message`. 171 172 ### General use of the API 173 174 Outlined below are some general use cases of the API. 175 176 #### Message handlers 177 A message handler is a function that is decorated with the `message_handler` decorator of a TeleBot instance. Message handlers consist of one or multiple filters. 178 Each filter must return True for a certain message in order for a message handler to become eligible to handle that message. A message handler is declared in the following way (provided `bot` is an instance of TeleBot): 179 ```python 180 @bot.message_handler(filters) 181 def function_name(message): 182 bot.reply_to(message, "This is a message handler") 183 ``` 184 `function_name` is not bound to any restrictions. Any function name is permitted with message handlers. The function must accept at most one argument, which will be the message that the function must handle. 185 `filters` is a list of keyword arguments. 186 A filter is declared in the following manner: `name=argument`. One handler may have multiple filters. 187 TeleBot supports the following filters: 188 189 |name|argument(s)|Condition| 190 |:---:|---| ---| 191 |content_types|list of strings (default `['text']`)|`True` if message.content_type is in the list of strings.| 192 |regexp|a regular expression as a string|`True` if `re.search(regexp_arg)` returns `True` and `message.content_type == 'text'` (See [Python Regular Expressions](https://docs.python.org/2/library/re.html))| 193 |commands|list of strings|`True` if `message.content_type == 'text'` and `message.text` starts with a command that is in the list of strings.| 194 |chat_types|list of chat types|`True` if `message.chat.type` in your filter| 195 |func|a function (lambda or function reference)|`True` if the lambda or function reference returns `True`| 196 197 Here are some examples of using the filters and message handlers: 198 199 ```python 200 import telebot 201 bot = telebot.TeleBot("TOKEN") 202 203 # Handles all text messages that contains the commands '/start' or '/help'. 204 @bot.message_handler(commands=['start', 'help']) 205 def handle_start_help(message): 206 pass 207 208 # Handles all sent documents and audio files 209 @bot.message_handler(content_types=['document', 'audio']) 210 def handle_docs_audio(message): 211 pass 212 213 # Handles all text messages that match the regular expression 214 @bot.message_handler(regexp="SOME_REGEXP") 215 def handle_message(message): 216 pass 217 218 # Handles all messages for which the lambda returns True 219 @bot.message_handler(func=lambda message: message.document.mime_type == 'text/plain', content_types=['document']) 220 def handle_text_doc(message): 221 pass 222 223 # Which could also be defined as: 224 def test_message(message): 225 return message.document.mime_type == 'text/plain' 226 227 @bot.message_handler(func=test_message, content_types=['document']) 228 def handle_text_doc(message): 229 pass 230 231 # Handlers can be stacked to create a function which will be called if either message_handler is eligible 232 # This handler will be called if the message starts with '/hello' OR is some emoji 233 @bot.message_handler(commands=['hello']) 234 @bot.message_handler(func=lambda msg: msg.text.encode("utf-8") == SOME_FANCY_EMOJI) 235 def send_something(message): 236 pass 237 ``` 238 **Important: all handlers are tested in the order in which they were declared** 239 240 #### Edited Message handler 241 Handle edited messages 242 `@bot.edited_message_handler(filters) # <- passes a Message type object to your function` 243 244 #### Channel Post handler 245 Handle channel post messages 246 `@bot.channel_post_handler(filters) # <- passes a Message type object to your function` 247 248 #### Edited Channel Post handler 249 Handle edited channel post messages 250 `@bot.edited_channel_post_handler(filters) # <- passes a Message type object to your function` 251 252 #### Callback Query Handler 253 Handle callback queries 254 ```python 255 @bot.callback_query_handler(func=lambda call: True) 256 def test_callback(call): # <- passes a CallbackQuery type object to your function 257 logger.info(call) 258 ``` 259 260 #### Shipping Query Handler 261 Handle shipping queries 262 `@bot.shipping_query_handler() # <- passes a ShippingQuery type object to your function` 263 264 #### Pre Checkout Query Handler 265 Handle pre checkout queries 266 `@bot.pre_checkout_query_handler() # <- passes a PreCheckoutQuery type object to your function` 267 268 #### Poll Handler 269 Handle poll updates 270 `@bot.poll_handler() # <- passes a Poll type object to your function` 271 272 #### Poll Answer Handler 273 Handle poll answers 274 `@bot.poll_answer_handler() # <- passes a PollAnswer type object to your function` 275 276 #### My Chat Member Handler 277 Handle updates of a the bot's member status in a chat 278 `@bot.my_chat_member_handler() # <- passes a ChatMemberUpdated type object to your function` 279 280 #### Chat Member Handler 281 Handle updates of a chat member's status in a chat 282 `@bot.chat_member_handler() # <- passes a ChatMemberUpdated type object to your function` 283 *Note: "chat_member" updates are not requested by default. If you want to allow all update types, set `allowed_updates` in `bot.polling()` / `bot.infinity_polling()` to `util.update_types`* 284 285 #### Chat Join Request Handler 286 Handle chat join requests using: 287 `@bot.chat_join_request_handler() # <- passes ChatInviteLink type object to your function` 288 289 ### Inline Mode 290 291 More information about [Inline mode](https://core.telegram.org/bots/inline). 292 293 #### Inline handler 294 295 Now, you can use inline_handler to get inline queries in telebot. 296 297 ```python 298 299 @bot.inline_handler(lambda query: query.query == 'text') 300 def query_text(inline_query): 301 # Query message is text 302 ``` 303 304 #### Chosen Inline handler 305 306 Use chosen_inline_handler to get chosen_inline_result in telebot. Don't forget to add the /setinlinefeedback 307 command for @Botfather. 308 309 More information : [collecting-feedback](https://core.telegram.org/bots/inline#collecting-feedback) 310 311 ```python 312 @bot.chosen_inline_handler(func=lambda chosen_inline_result: True) 313 def test_chosen(chosen_inline_result): 314 # Process all chosen_inline_result. 315 ``` 316 317 #### Answer Inline Query 318 319 ```python 320 @bot.inline_handler(lambda query: query.query == 'text') 321 def query_text(inline_query): 322 try: 323 r = types.InlineQueryResultArticle('1', 'Result', types.InputTextMessageContent('Result message.')) 324 r2 = types.InlineQueryResultArticle('2', 'Result2', types.InputTextMessageContent('Result message2.')) 325 bot.answer_inline_query(inline_query.id, [r, r2]) 326 except Exception as e: 327 print(e) 328 329 ``` 330 331 ### Additional API features 332 333 #### Middleware Handlers 334 335 A middleware handler is a function that allows you to modify requests or the bot context as they pass through the 336 Telegram to the bot. You can imagine middleware as a chain of logic connection handled before any other handlers are 337 executed. Middleware processing is disabled by default, enable it by setting `apihelper.ENABLE_MIDDLEWARE = True`. 338 339 ```python 340 apihelper.ENABLE_MIDDLEWARE = True 341 342 @bot.middleware_handler(update_types=['message']) 343 def modify_message(bot_instance, mesDue to the max output limit, only part of this file has been shown to you. <ROLE>
Your primary role is to assist users by executing commands, modifying code, and solving technical problems effectively. You should be thorough, methodical, and prioritize quality over speed.

* Each action you take is somewhat expensive. Wherever possible, combine multiple actions into a single action, e.g. combine multiple bash commands into one, using sed and grep to edit/view multiple files at once.

</FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>

* When implementing solutions, focus on making the minimal changes needed to solve the problem.
* Before implementing any changes, first thoroughly understand the codebase through exploration.
* If you are adding a lot of code to a function or file, consider splitting the function or file into smaller pieces when appropriate.
</CODE_QUALITY>

<VERSION_CONTROL>
* When configuring git credentials, use "openhands" as the user.name and "openhands@all-hands.dev" as the user.email by default, unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* Exercise caution with git operations. Do NOT make potentially dangerous changes (e.g., pushing to main, deleting repositories) unless explicitly asked to do so.
* When committing changes, use `git status` to see all modified files, and stage all files necessary for the commit. Use `git commit -a` whenever possible.
* Do NOT commit files that typically shouldn't go into version control (e.g., node_modules/, .env files, build directories, cache files, large binaries) unless explicitly instructed by the user.
* If unsure about committing certain files, check for the presence of .gitignore files or ask the user for clarification.
</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PULL_REQUESTS>
* When creating pull requests, create only ONE per session/issue unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* When working with an existing PR, update it with new commits rather than creating additional PRs for the same issue.
* When updating a PR, preserve the original PR title and purpose, updating description only when necessary.
</PULL_REQUESTS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>
1. EXPLORATION: Thoroughly explore relevant files and understand the context before proposing solutions
2. ANALYSIS: Consider multiple approaches and select the most promising one
3. TESTING:
   * For bug fixes: Create tests to verify issues before implementing fixes
   * For new features: Consider test-driven development when appropriate
   * If the repository lacks testing infrastructure and implementing tests would require extensive setup, consult with the user before investing time in building testing infrastructure
   * If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to install all dependencies
4. IMPLEMENTATION: Make focused, minimal changes to address the problem
5. VERIFICATION: If the environment is set up to run tests, test your implementation thoroughly, including edge cases. If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to run tests.
</PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

<SECURITY>
* Only use GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in ways the user has explicitly requested and would expect.
* Use APIs to work with GitHub or other platforms, unless the user asks otherwise or your task requires browsing.
</SECURITY>

<ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>
* When user asks you to run an application, don't stop if the application is not installed. Instead, please install the application and run the command again.
* If you encounter missing dependencies:
  1. First, look around in the repository for existing dependency files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, Gemfile, etc.)
  2. If dependency files exist, use them to install all dependencies at once (e.g., `pip install -r requirements.txt`, `npm install`, etc.)
  3. Only install individual packages directly if no dependency files are found or if only specific packages are needed
* Similarly, if you encounter missing dependencies for essential tools requested by the user, install them when possible.
</ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>

<TROUBLESHOOTING>
* If you've made repeated attempts to solve a problem but tests still fail or the user reports it's still broken:
  1. Step back and reflect on 5-7 different possible sources of the problem
  2. Assess the likelihood of each possible cause
  3. Methodically address the most likely causes, starting with the highest probability
  4. Document your reasoning process
* When you run into any major issue while executing a plan from the user, please don't try to directly work around it. Instead, propose a new plan and confirm with the user before proceeding.
</TROUBLESHOOTING>

function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.
* Persistent session: Commands execute in a persistent shell session where environment variables, virtual environments, and working directory persist between commands.
* Timeout: Commands have a soft timeout of 10 seconds, once that's reached, you have the option to continue or interrupt the command (see section below for details)

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* Long running commands: For commands that may run indefinitely, run them in the background and redirect output to a file, e.g. `python3 app.py > server.log 2>&1 &`. For commands that need to run for a specific duration, like "sleep", you can set the "timeout" argument to specify a hard timeout in seconds.
* Interact with running process: If a bash command returns exit code `-1`, this means the process is not yet finished. By setting `is_input` to `true`, you can:
  - Send empty `command` to retrieve additional logs
  - Send text (set `command` to the text) to STDIN of the running process
  - Send control commands like `C-c` (Ctrl+C), `C-d` (Ctrl+D), or `C-z` (Ctrl+Z) to interrupt the process

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Before creating new directories or files, first verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
* Directory management: Try to maintain working directory by using absolute paths and avoiding excessive use of `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output truncation: If the output exceeds a maximum length, it will be truncated before being returned.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional. Sets a hard timeout in seconds for the command execution. If not provided, the command will use the default soft timeout behavior.

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: finish
function.description:
Signals the completion of the current task or conversation.

Use this tool when:
- You have successfully completed the user's requested task
- You cannot proceed further due to technical limitations or missing information

The message should include:
- A clear summary of actions taken and their results
- Any next steps for the user
- Explanation if you're unable to complete the task
- Any follow-up questions if more information is needed

The task_completed field should be set to True if you believed you have completed the task, and False otherwise.

message.description: Final message to send to the user

task_completed.description: Whether you have completed the task.

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw
