     1	django-helpdesk - A Django powered ticket tracker for small businesses.
     2	=======================================================================
     3	
     4	.. image:: https://dev.azure.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/_apis/build/status/django-helpdesk.django-helpdesk?branchName=master
     5	  :target: https://dev.azure.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/_build/latest?definitionId=1&branchName=master
     6	  :alt: Build Status
     7	
     8	.. image:: https://codecov.io/gh/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/branch/develop/graph/badge.svg
     9	  :target: https://codecov.io/gh/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk
    10	
    11	Copyright 2009-2025 Ross Poulton and django-helpdesk contributors. All Rights Reserved.
    12	See LICENSE for details.
    13	
    14	django-helpdesk was formerly known as Jutda Helpdesk, named after the
    15	company which originally created it. As of January 2011 the name has been
    16	changed to reflect what it really is: a Django-powered ticket tracker with
    17	contributors reaching far beyond Jutda.
    18	
    19	Complete documentation is available in the docs/ directory,
    20	or online at http://django-helpdesk.readthedocs.org/.
    21	
    22	
    23	Demo Quickstart
    24	---------------
    25	
    26	`django-helpdesk` includes a basic demo Django project so that you may easily
    27	get started with testing or developing `django-helpdesk`. The demo project
    28	resides in the `demo/` top-level folder.
    29	
    30	It's likely that you can start up a demo project server by running
    31	only the command (consider creating a virtualenv before):
    32	
    33	    make rundemo
    34	
    35	or with docker::
    36	
    37	    docker build . -t demodesk
    38	    docker run --rm -v "$PWD:/app" -p 8080:8080 demodesk
    39	
    40	then pointing your web browser at http://localhost:8080 (log in as user
    41	`admin`` with password `Test1234`).
    42	
    43	For more information and options, please read the `demo/README.rst` file.
    44	
    45	**NOTE REGARDING SQLITE AND SEARCHING:**
    46	The demo project uses `sqlite` as its database. Sqlite does not allow
    47	case-insensitive searches and so the search function may not work as
    48	effectively as it would on other database such as PostgreSQL or MySQL
    49	that does support case-insensitive searches.
    50	For more information, see this note_ in the Django documentation.
    51	
    52	When you try to do a keyword search using `sqlite`, a message will be displayed
    53	to alert you to this shortcoming. There is no way around it, sorry.
    54	
    55	Installation
    56	------------
    57	
    58	* |standalone_icon| For **standalone** installation, refer to `standalone documentation <./docs/standalone.rst>`_.
    59	
    60	* |django_icon| To **integrate** with an existing Django application, follow the guidelines in `installation documentation <./docs/install.rst>`_ and `configuration documentation <./docs/configuration.rst>`_.
    61	
    62	.. |standalone_icon| image:: helpdesk/static/helpdesk/img/icon512.png
    63	   :height: 24px
    64	   :width: 24px
    65	   :align: middle
    66	
    67	.. |django_icon| image:: helpdesk/static/helpdesk/img/django-logo-positive.png
    68	   :height: 24px
    69	   :width: 60px
    70	   :align: middle
    71	
    72	Developer Environment
    73	---------------------
    74	
    75	Follow these steps to set up your development environment to contribute to helpdesk:
    76	 - check out the helpdesk app to your local file system::
    77	
    78	    git clone https://github.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk.git
    79	 
    80	 - install a virtual environment and activate it::
    81	  
    82	    python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
    83	
    84	 - install the requirements for development::
    85	
    86	    pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt
    87	
    88	 - you can install the requirements for testing as well::
    89	
    90	    pip install -r requirements-testing.txt
    91	
    92	To deactivate the virtual environment, use ``deactivate``. Then to reactivate it, just run::
    93	
    94	    source .venv/bin/activate
    95	
    96	To see option for the Makefile run: `make`
    97	
    98	The project enforces a standardized formatting in the CI/CD pipeline. To ensure you have the correct formatting run::
    99	
   100	    make checkformat
   101	    
   102	To auto format any code use this::
   103	
   104	    make format
   105	
   106	Testing
   107	-------
   108	
   109	From the command line you can run the tests using: `make test`
   110	
   111	See `quicktest.py` for usage details.
   112	
   113	If you need to create tests for new features, add your tests in a test file to the `tests` module and call them in the test VENV with::
   114	    python quicktest.py helpdesk.tests.test_my_new_features -v 2
   115	
   116	Upgrading from previous versions
   117	--------------------------------
   118	
   119	If you are upgrading from a previous version of `django-helpdesk` that used
   120	migrations, get an up to date version of the code base (eg by using
   121	`git pull` or `pip install --upgrade django-helpdesk`) then migrate the database::
   122	
   123	    python manage.py migrate helpdesk --db-dry-run # DB untouched
   124	    python manage.py migrate helpdesk
   125	
   126	Lastly, restart your web server software (eg Apache) or FastCGI instance, to
   127	ensure the latest changes are in use.
   128	
   129	Unfortunately we are unable to assist if you are upgrading from a
   130	version of `django-helpdesk` prior to migrations (ie pre-2011).
   131	
   132	You can continue to the 'Initial Configuration' area, if needed.
   133	
   134	Contributing
   135	------------
   136	
   137	We're happy to include any type of contribution! This can be:
   138	
   139	* back-end python/django code development
   140	* front-end web development (HTML/Javascript, especially jQuery)
   141	* language translations
   142	* writing improved documentation and demos
   143	
   144	For more information on contributing, please see the `CONTRIBUTING.rst` file.
   145	
   146	
   147	Licensing
   148	---------
   149	
   150	django-helpdesk is licensed under terms of the BSD 3-clause license.
   151	See the `LICENSE` file for full licensing terms.
   152	
   153	Note that django-helpdesk is distributed with 3rd party products which
   154	have their own licenses. See LICENSE.3RDPARTY for license terms for
   155	included packages.
   156	
   157	.. _note: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/databases/#substring-matching-and-case-sensitivity
   158

history.content:
django-helpdesk make shortcuts
Here are available targets:
* help - Display callable targets.
* develop - Install minimal development utilities for Python3.
* clean - Basic cleanup, mostly temporary files.
* distclean - Remove local builds, such as *.egg-info.
* maintainer-clean - Remove almost everything that can be re-generated.
* test - Run test suites.
* format - Run the PEP8 formatter.
* checkformat - checks formatting against configured format specifications for the project.
* documentation - Build documentation (Sphinx, README, ...).
* sphinx - Build Sphinx documentation (docs).
* readme - Build standalone documentation files (README, CONTRIBUTING...).
* demo - Setup demo project using Python3.
* rundemo - Run demo server using Python3.
* migrations - Create Django migrations for this project.
* release - Tag and push to PyPI.

history.message:
You are OpenHands agent, a helpful AI assistant that can interact with a computer to solve tasks.

<ROLE>
Your primary role is to assist users by executing commands, modifying code, and solving technical problems effectively. You should be thorough, methodical, and prioritize quality over speed.
* If the user asks a question, like "why is X happening", don't try to fix the problem. Just give an answer to the question.
</ROLE>

<EFFICIENCY>
* Each action you take is somewhat expensive. Wherever possible, combine multiple actions into a single action, e.g. combine multiple bash commands into one, using sed and grep to edit/view multiple files at once.
* When exploring the codebase, use efficient tools like find, grep, and git commands with appropriate filters to minimize unnecessary operations.
</EFFICIENCY>

<FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>
* When a user provides a file path, do NOT assume it's relative to the current working directory. First explore the file system to locate the file before working on it.
* If asked to edit a file, edit the file directly, rather than creating a new file with a different filename.
* For global search-and-replace operations, consider using `sed` instead of opening file editors multiple times.
</FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>

<CODE_QUALITY>
* Write clean, efficient code with minimal comments. Avoid redundancy in comments: Do not repeat information that can be easily inferred from the code itself.
* When implementing solutions, focus on making the minimal changes needed to solve the problem.
* Before implementing any changes, first thoroughly understand the codebase through exploration.
* If you are adding a lot of code to a function or file, consider splitting the function or file into smaller pieces when appropriate.
</CODE_QUALITY>

<VERSION_CONTROL>
* When configuring git credentials, use "openhands" as the user.name and "openhands@all-hands.dev" as the user.email by default, unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* Exercise caution with git operations. Do NOT make potentially dangerous changes (e.g., pushing to main, deleting repositories) unless explicitly asked to do so.
* When committing changes, use `git status` to see all modified files, and stage all files necessary for the commit. Use `git commit -a` whenever possible.
* Do NOT commit files that typically shouldn't go into version control (e.g., node_modules/, .env files, build directories, cache files, large binaries) unless explicitly instructed by the user.
* If unsure about committing certain files, check for the presence of .gitignore files or ask the user for clarification.
</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PULL_REQUESTS>
* When creating pull requests, create only ONE per session/issue unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* When working with an existing PR, update it with new commits rather than creating additional PRs for the same issue.
* When updating a PR, preserve the original PR title and purpose, updating description only when necessary.
</PULL_REQUESTS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>
1. EXPLORATION: Thoroughly explore relevant files and understand the context before proposing solutions
2. ANALYSIS: Consider multiple approaches and select the most promising one
3. TESTING:
   * For bug fixes: Create tests to verify issues before implementing fixes
   * For new features: Consider test-driven development when appropriate
   * If the repository lacks testing infrastructure and implementing tests would require extensive setup, consult with the user before investing time in building testing infrastructure
   * If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to install all dependencies
4. IMPLEMENTATION: Make focused, minimal changes to address the problem
5. VERIFICATION: If the environment is set up to run tests, test your implementation thoroughly, including edge cases. If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to run tests.
</PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

<SECURITY>
* Only use GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in ways the user has explicitly requested and would expect.
* Use APIs to work with GitHub or other platforms, unless the user asks otherwise or your task requires browsing.
</SECURITY>

<ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>
* When user asks you to run an application, don't stop if the application is not installed. Instead, please install the application and run the command again.
* If you encounter missing dependencies:
  1. First, look around in the repository for existing dependency files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, Gemfile, etc.)
  2. If dependency files exist, use them to install all dependencies at once (e.g., `pip install -r requirements.txt`, `npm install`, etc.)
  3. Only install individual packages directly if no dependency files are found or if only specific packages are needed
* Similarly, if you encounter missing dependencies for essential tools requested by the user, install them when possible.
</ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>

<TROUBLESHOOTING>
* If you've made repeated attempts to solve a problem but tests still fail or the user reports it's still broken:
  1. Step back and reflect on 5-7 different possible sources of the problem
  2. Assess the likelihood of each possible cause
  3. Methodically address the most likely causes, starting with the highest probability
  4. Document your reasoning process
* When you run into any major issue while executing a plan from the user, please don't try to directly work around it. Instead, propose a new plan and confirm with the user before proceeding.
</TROUBLESHOOTING>

function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.
* Persistent session: Commands execute in a persistent shell session where environment variables, virtual environments, and working directory persist between commands.
* Timeout: Commands have a soft timeout of 10 seconds, once that's reached, you have the option to continue or interrupt the command (see section below for details)

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* Long running commands: For commands that may run indefinitely, run them in the background and redirect output to a file, e.g. `python3 app.py > server.log 2>&1 &`. For commands that need to run for a specific duration, like "sleep", you can set the "timeout" argument to specify a hard timeout in seconds.
* Interact with running process: If a bash command returns exit code `-1`, this means the process is not yet finished. By setting `is_input` to `true`, you can:
  - Send empty `command` to retrieve additional logs
  - Send text (set `command` to the text) to STDIN of the running process
  - Send control commands like `C-c` (Ctrl+C), `C-d` (Ctrl+D), or `C-z` (Ctrl+Z) to interrupt the process

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Before creating new directories or files, first verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
* Directory management: Try to maintain working directory by using absolute paths and avoiding excessive use of `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output truncation: If the output exceeds a maximum length, it will be truncated before being returned.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional. Sets a hard timeout in seconds for the command execution. If not provided, the command will use the default soft timeout behavior.

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: finish
function.description:
Signals the completion of the current task or conversation.

Use this tool when:
- You have successfully completed the user's requested task
- You cannot proceed further due to technical limitations or missing information

The message should include:
- A clear summary of actions taken and their results
- Any next steps for the user
- Explanation if you're unable to complete the task
- Any follow-up questions if more information is needed

The task_completed field should be set to True if you believed you have completed the task, and False otherwise.

message.description: Final message to send to the user

task_completed.description: Whether you have completed the task.

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw

history.message:
You are OpenHands agent, a helpful AI assistant that can interact with a computer to solve tasks.

<ROLE>
Your primary role is to assist users by executing commands, modifying code, and solving technical problems effectively. You should be thorough, methodical, and prioritize quality over speed.
* If the user asks a question, like "why is X happening", don't try to fix the problem. Just give an answer to the question.
</ROLE>

<EFFICIENCY>
* Each action you take is somewhat expensive. Wherever possible, combine multiple actions into a single action, e.g. combine multiple bash commands into one, using sed and grep to edit/view multiple files at once.
* When exploring the codebase, use efficient tools like find, grep, and git commands with appropriate filters to minimize unnecessary operations.
</EFFICIENCY>

<FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>
* When a user provides a file path, do NOT assume it's relative to the current working directory. First explore the file system to locate the file before working on it.
* If asked to edit a file, edit the file directly, rather than creating a new file with a different filename.
* For global search-and-replace operations, consider using `sed` instead of opening file editors multiple times.
</FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>

<CODE_QUALITY>
* Write clean, efficient code with minimal comments. Avoid redundancy in comments: Do not repeat information that can be easily inferred from the code itself.
* When implementing solutions, focus on making the minimal changes needed to solve the problem.
* Before implementing any changes, first thoroughly understand the codebase through exploration.
* If you are adding a lot of code to a function or file, consider splitting the function or file into smaller pieces when appropriate.
</CODE_QUALITY>

<VERSION_CONTROL>
* When configuring git credentials, use "openhands" as the user.name and "openhands@all-hands.dev" as the user.email by default, unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* Exercise caution with git operations. Do NOT make potentially dangerous changes (e.g., pushing to main, deleting repositories) unless explicitly asked to do so.
* When committing changes, use `git status` to see all modified files, and stage all files necessary for the commit. Use `git commit -a` whenever possible.
* Do NOT commit files that typically shouldn't go into version control (e.g., node_modules/, .env files, build directories, cache files, large binaries) unless explicitly instructed by the user.
* If unsure about committing certain files, check for the presence of .gitignore files or ask the user for clarification.
</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PULL_REQUESTS>
* When creating pull requests, create only ONE per session/issue unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* When working with an existing PR, update it with new commits rather than creating additional PRs for the same issue.
* When updating a PR, preserve the original PR title and purpose, updating description only when necessary.
</PULL_REQUESTS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>
1. EXPLORATION: Thoroughly explore relevant files and understand the context before proposing solutions
2. ANALYSIS: Consider multiple approaches and select the most promising one
3. TESTING:
   * For bug fixes: Create tests to verify issues before implementing fixes
   * For new features: Consider test-driven development when appropriate
   * If the repository lacks testing infrastructure and implementing tests would require extensive setup, consult with the user before investing time in building testing infrastructure
   * If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to install all dependencies
4. IMPLEMENTATION: Make focused, minimal changes to address the problem
5. VERIFICATION: If the environment is set up to run tests, test your implementation thoroughly, including edge cases. If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to run tests.
</PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

<SECURITY>
* Only use GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in ways the user has explicitly requested and would expect.
* Use APIs to work with GitHub or other platforms, unless the user asks otherwise or your task requires browsing.
</SECURITY>

<ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>
* When user asks you to run an application, don't stop if the application is not installed. Instead, please install the application and run the command again.
* If you encounter missing dependencies:
  1. First, look around in the repository for existing dependency files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, Gemfile, etc.)
  2. If dependency files exist, use them to install all dependencies at once (e.g., `pip install -r requirements.txt`, `npm install`, etc.)
  3. Only install individual packages directly if no dependency files are found or if only specific packages are needed
* Similarly, if you encounter missing dependencies for essential tools requested by the user, install them when possible.
</ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>

<TROUBLESHOOTING>
* If you've made repeated attempts to solve a problem but tests still fail or the user reports it's still broken:
  1. Step back and reflect on 5-7 different possible sources of the problem
  2. Assess the likelihood of each possible cause
  3. Methodically address the most likely causes, starting with the highest probability
  4. Document your reasoning process
* When you run into any major issue while executing a plan from the user, please don't try to directly work around it. Instead, propose a new plan and confirm with the user before proceeding.
</TROUBLESHOOTING>

args.content:
You are OpenHands agent, a helpful AI assistant that can interact with a computer to solve tasks.

<ROLE>
Your primary role is to assist users by executing commands, modifying code, and solving technical problems effectively. You should be thorough, methodical, and prioritize quality over speed.
* If the user asks a question, like "why is X happening", don't try to fix the problem. Just give an answer to the question.
</ROLE>

<EFFICIENCY>
* Each action you take is somewhat expensive. Wherever possible, combine multiple actions into a single action, e.g. combine multiple bash commands into one, using sed and grep to edit/view multiple files at once.
* When exploring the codebase, use efficient tools like find, grep, and git commands with appropriate filters to minimize unnecessary operations.
</EFFICIENCY>

<FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>
* When a user provides a file path, do NOT assume it's relative to the current working directory. First explore the file system to locate the file before working on it.
* If asked to edit a file, edit the file directly, rather than creating a new file with a different filename.
* For global search-and-replace operations, consider using `sed` instead of opening file editors multiple times.
</FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>

<CODE_QUALITY>
* Write clean, efficient code with minimal comments. Avoid redundancy in comments: Do not repeat information that can be easily inferred from the code itself.
* When implementing solutions, focus on making the minimal changes needed to solve the problem.
* Before implementing any changes, first thoroughly understand the codebase through exploration.
* If you are adding a lot of code to a function or file, consider splitting the function or file into smaller pieces when appropriate.
</CODE_QUALITY>

<VERSION_CONTROL>
* When configuring git credentials, use "openhands" as the user.name and "openhands@all-hands.dev" as the user.email by default, unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* Exercise caution with git operations. Do NOT make potentially dangerous changes (e.g., pushing to main, deleting repositories) unless explicitly asked to do so.
* When committing changes, use `git status` to see all modified files, and stage all files necessary for the commit. Use `git commit -a` whenever possible.
* Do NOT commit files that typically shouldn't go into version control (e.g., node_modules/, .env files, build directories, cache files, large binaries) unless explicitly instructed by the user.
* If unsure about committing certain files, check for the presence of .gitignore files or ask the user for clarification.
</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PULL_REQUESTS>
* When creating pull requests, create only ONE per session/issue unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* When working with an existing PR, update it with new commits rather than creating additional PRs for the same issue.
* When updating a PR, preserve the original PR title and purpose, updating description only when necessary.
</PULL_REQUESTS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>
1. EXPLORATION: Thoroughly explore relevant files and understand the context before proposing solutions
2. ANALYSIS: Consider multiple approaches and select the most promising one
3. TESTING:
   * For bug fixes: Create tests to verify issues before implementing fixes
   * For new features: Consider test-driven development when appropriate
   * If the repository lacks testing infrastructure and implementing tests would require extensive setup, consult with the user before investing time in building testing infrastructure
   * If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to install all dependencies
4. IMPLEMENTATION: Make focused, minimal changes to address the problem
5. VERIFICATION: If the environment is set up to run tests, test your implementation thoroughly, including edge cases. If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to run tests.
</PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

<SECURITY>
* Only use GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in ways the user has explicitly requested and would expect.
* Use APIs to work with GitHub or other platforms, unless the user asks otherwise or your task requires browsing.
</SECURITY>

<ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>
* When user asks you to run an application, don't stop if the application is not installed. Instead, please install the application and run the command again.
* If you encounter missing dependencies:
  1. First, look around in the repository for existing dependency files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, Gemfile, etc.)
  2. If dependency files exist, use them to install all dependencies at once (e.g., `pip install -r requirements.txt`, `npm install`, etc.)
  3. Only install individual packages directly if no dependency files are found or if only specific packages are needed
* Similarly, if you encounter missing dependencies for essential tools requested by the user, install them when possible.
</ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>

<TROUBLESHOOTING>
* If you've made repeated attempts to solve a problem but tests still fail or the user reports it's still broken:
  1. Step back and reflect on 5-7 different possible sources of the problem
  2. Assess the likelihood of each possible cause
  3. Methodically address the most likely causes, starting with the highest probability
  4. Document your reasoning process
* When you run into any major issue while executing a plan from the user, please don't try to directly work around it. Instead, propose a new plan and confirm with the user before proceeding.
</TROUBLESHOOTING>

function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.
* Persistent session: Commands execute in a persistent shell session where environment variables, virtual environments, and working directory persist between commands.
* Timeout: Commands have a soft timeout of 10 seconds, once that's reached, you have the option to continue or interrupt the command (see section below for details)

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* Long running commands: For commands that may run indefinitely, run them in the background and redirect output to a file, e.g. `python3 app.py > server.log 2>&1 &`. For commands that need to run for a specific duration, like "sleep", you can set the "timeout" argument to specify a hard timeout in seconds.
* Interact with running process: If a bash command returns exit code `-1`, this means the process is not yet finished. By setting `is_input` to `true`, you can:
  - Send empty `command` to retrieve additional logs
  - Send text (set `command` to the text) to STDIN of the running process
  - Send control commands like `C-c` (Ctrl+C), `C-d` (Ctrl+D), or `C-z` (Ctrl+Z) to interrupt the process

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Before creating new directories or files, first verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
* Directory management: Try to maintain working directory by using absolute paths and avoiding excessive use of `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output truncation: If the output exceeds a maximum length, it will be truncated before being returned.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional. Sets a hard timeout in seconds for the command execution. If not provided, the command will use the default soft timeout behavior.

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: finish
function.description:
Signals the completion of the current task or conversation.

Use this tool when:
- You have successfully completed the user's requested task
- You cannot proceed further due to technical limitations or missing information

The message should include:
- A clear summary of actions taken and their results
- Any next steps for the user
- Explanation if you're unable to complete the task
- Any follow-up questions if more information is needed

The task_completed field should be set to True if you believed you have completed the task, and False otherwise.

message.description: Final message to send to the user

task_completed.description: Whether you have completed the task.

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw

history.content:
Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /workspace/python-tabulate/README.md: 1 python-tabulate 2 =============== 3 4 Pretty-print tabular data in Python, a library and a command-line 5 utility. 6 7 The main use cases of the library are: 8 9 - printing small tables without hassle: just one function call, 10 formatting is guided by the data itself 11 - authoring tabular data for lightweight plain-text markup: multiple 12 output formats suitable for further editing or transformation 13 - readable presentation of mixed textual and numeric data: smart 14 column alignment, configurable number formatting, alignment by a 15 decimal point 16 17 Installation 18 ------------ 19 20 To install the Python library and the command line utility, run: 21 22 ```shell 23 pip install tabulate 24 ``` 25 26 The command line utility will be installed as `tabulate` to `bin` on 27 Linux (e.g. `/usr/bin`); or as `tabulate.exe` to `Scripts` in your 28 Python installation on Windows (e.g. `C:\Python39\Scripts\tabulate.exe`). 29 30 You may consider installing the library only for the current user: 31 32 ```shell 33 pip install tabulate --user 34 ``` 35 36 In this case the command line utility will be installed to 37 `~/.local/bin/tabulate` on Linux and to 38 `%APPDATA%\Python\Scripts\tabulate.exe` on Windows. 39 40 To install just the library on Unix-like operating systems: 41 42 ```shell 43 TABULATE_INSTALL=lib-only pip install tabulate 44 ``` 45 46 On Windows: 47 48 ```shell 49 set TABULATE_INSTALL=lib-only 50 pip install tabulate 51 ``` 52 53 Build status 54 ------------ 55 56 [![python-tabulate](https://github.com/astanin/python-tabulate/actions/workflows/tabulate.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/astanin/python-tabulate/actions/workflows/tabulate.yml) 57 58 Library usage 59 ------------- 60 61 The module provides just one function, `tabulate`, which takes a list of 62 lists or another tabular data type as the first argument, and outputs a 63 nicely formatted plain-text table: 64 65 ```pycon 66 >>> from tabulate import tabulate 67 68 >>> table = [["Sun",696000,1989100000],["Earth",6371,5973.6], 69 ... ["Moon",1737,73.5],["Mars",3390,641.85]] 70 >>> print(tabulate(table)) 71 ----- ------ ------------- 72 Sun 696000 1.9891e+09 73 Earth 6371 5973.6 74 Moon 1737 73.5 75 Mars 3390 641.85 76 ----- ------ ------------- 77 78 ``` 79 80 The following tabular data types are supported: 81 82 - list of lists or another iterable of iterables 83 - list or another iterable of dicts (keys as columns) 84 - dict of iterables (keys as columns) 85 - list of dataclasses (field names as columns) 86 - two-dimensional NumPy array 87 - NumPy record arrays (names as columns) 88 - pandas.DataFrame 89 90 Tabulate is a Python3 library. 91 92 ### Headers 93 94 The second optional argument named `headers` defines a list of column 95 headers to be used: 96 97 ```pycon 98 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers=["Planet","R (km)", "mass (x 10^29 kg)"])) 99 Planet R (km) mass (x 10^29 kg) 100 -------- -------- ------------------- 101 Sun 696000 1.9891e+09 102 Earth 6371 5973.6 103 Moon 1737 73.5 104 Mars 3390 641.85 105 106 ``` 107 108 If `headers="firstrow"`, then the first row of data is used: 109 110 ```pycon 111 >>> print(tabulate([["Name","Age"],["Alice",24],["Bob",19]], 112 ... headers="firstrow")) 113 Name Age 114 ------ ----- 115 Alice 24 116 Bob 19 117 118 ``` 119 120 If `headers="keys"`, then the keys of a dictionary/dataframe, or column 121 indices are used. It also works for NumPy record arrays and lists of 122 dictionaries or named tuples: 123 124 ```pycon 125 >>> print(tabulate({"Name": ["Alice", "Bob"], 126 ... "Age": [24, 19]}, headers="keys")) 127 Name Age 128 ------ ----- 129 Alice 24 130 Bob 19 131 132 ``` 133 134 When data is a list of dictionaries, a dictionary can be passed as `headers` 135 to replace the keys with other column labels: 136 137 ```pycon 138 >>> print(tabulate([{1: "Alice", 2: 24}, {1: "Bob", 2: 19}], 139 ... headers={1: "Name", 2: "Age"})) 140 Name Age 141 ------ ----- 142 Alice 24 143 Bob 19 144 145 ``` 146 147 ### Row Indices 148 149 By default, only pandas.DataFrame tables have an additional column 150 called row index. To add a similar column to any other type of table, 151 pass `showindex="always"` or `showindex=True` argument to `tabulate()`. 152 To suppress row indices for all types of data, pass `showindex="never"` 153 or `showindex=False`. To add a custom row index column, pass 154 `showindex=rowIDs`, where `rowIDs` is some iterable: 155 156 ```pycon 157 >>> print(tabulate([["F",24],["M",19]], showindex="always")) 158 - - -- 159 0 F 24 160 1 M 19 161 - - -- 162 163 ``` 164 165 ### Table format 166 167 There is more than one way to format a table in plain text. The third 168 optional argument named `tablefmt` defines how the table is formatted. 169 170 Supported table formats are: 171 172 - "plain" 173 - "simple" 174 - "github" 175 - "grid" 176 - "simple\_grid" 177 - "rounded\_grid" 178 - "heavy\_grid" 179 - "mixed\_grid" 180 - "double\_grid" 181 - "fancy\_grid" 182 - "outline" 183 - "simple\_outline" 184 - "rounded\_outline" 185 - "heavy\_outline" 186 - "mixed\_outline" 187 - "double\_outline" 188 - "fancy\_outline" 189 - "pipe" 190 - "orgtbl" 191 - "asciidoc" 192 - "jira" 193 - "presto" 194 - "pretty" 195 - "psql" 196 - "rst" 197 - "mediawiki" 198 - "moinmoin" 199 - "youtrack" 200 - "html" 201 - "unsafehtml" 202 - "latex" 203 - "latex\_raw" 204 - "latex\_booktabs" 205 - "latex\_longtable" 206 - "textile" 207 - "tsv" 208 209 `plain` tables do not use any pseudo-graphics to draw lines: 210 211 ```pycon 212 >>> table = [["spam",42],["eggs",451],["bacon",0]] 213 >>> headers = ["item", "qty"] 214 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="plain")) 215 item qty 216 spam 42 217 eggs 451 218 bacon 0 219 220 ``` 221 222 `simple` is the default format (the default may change in future 223 versions). It corresponds to `simple_tables` in [Pandoc Markdown 224 extensions](http://johnmacfarlane.net/pandoc/README.html#tables): 225 226 ```pycon 227 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="simple")) 228 item qty 229 ------ ----- 230 spam 42 231 eggs 451 232 bacon 0 233 234 ``` 235 236 `github` follows the conventions of GitHub flavored Markdown. It 237 corresponds to the `pipe` format without alignment colons: 238 239 ```pycon 240 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="github")) 241 | item | qty | 242 |--------|-------| 243 | spam | 42 | 244 | eggs | 451 | 245 | bacon | 0 | 246 247 ``` 248 249 `grid` is like tables formatted by Emacs' 250 [table.el](http://table.sourceforge.net/) package. It corresponds to 251 `grid_tables` in Pandoc Markdown extensions: 252 253 ```pycon 254 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="grid")) 255 +--------+-------+ 256 | item | qty | 257 +========+=======+ 258 | spam | 42 | 259 +--------+-------+ 260 | eggs | 451 | 261 +--------+-------+ 262 | bacon | 0 | 263 +--------+-------+ 264 265 ``` 266 267 `simple_grid` draws a grid using single-line box-drawing characters: 268 269 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="simple_grid")) 270 ┌────────┬───────┐ 271 │ item │ qty │ 272 ├────────┼───────┤ 273 │ spam │ 42 │ 274 ├────────┼───────┤ 275 │ eggs │ 451 │ 276 ├────────┼───────┤ 277 │ bacon │ 0 │ 278 └────────┴───────┘ 279 280 `rounded_grid` draws a grid using single-line box-drawing characters with rounded corners: 281 282 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="rounded_grid")) 283 ╭────────┬───────╮ 284 │ item │ qty │ 285 ├────────┼───────┤ 286 │ spam │ 42 │ 287 ├────────┼───────┤ 288 │ eggs │ 451 │ 289 ├────────┼───────┤ 290 │ bacon │ 0 │ 291 ╰────────┴───────╯ 292 293 `heavy_grid` draws a grid using bold (thick) single-line box-drawing characters: 294 295 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="heavy_grid")) 296 ┏━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━┓ 297 ┃ item ┃ qty ┃ 298 ┣━━━━━━━━╋━━━━━━━┫ 299 ┃ spam ┃ 42 ┃ 300 ┣━━━━━━━━╋━━━━━━━┫ 301 ┃ eggs ┃ 451 ┃ 302 ┣━━━━━━━━╋━━━━━━━┫ 303 ┃ bacon ┃ 0 ┃ 304 ┗━━━━━━━━┻━━━━━━━┛ 305 306 `mixed_grid` draws a grid using a mix of light (thin) and heavy (thick) lines box-drawing characters: 307 308 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="mixed_grid")) 309 ┍━━━━━━━━┯━━━━━━━┑ 310 │ item │ qty │ 311 ┝━━━━━━━━┿━━━━━━━┥ 312 │ spam │ 42 │ 313 ├────────┼───────┤ 314 │ eggs │ 451 │ 315 ├────────┼───────┤ 316 │ bacon │ 0 │ 317 ┕━━━━━━━━┷━━━━━━━┙ 318 319 `double_grid` draws a grid using double-line box-drawing characters: 320 321 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="double_grid")) 322 ╔════════╦═══════╗ 323 ║ item ║ qty ║ 324 ╠════════╬═══════╣ 325 ║ spam ║ 42 ║ 326 ╠════════╬═══════╣ 327 ║ eggs ║ 451 ║ 328 ╠════════╬═══════╣ 329 ║ bacon ║ 0 ║ 330 ╚════════╩═══════╝ 331 332 `fancy_grid` draws a grid using a mix of single and 333 double-line box-drawing characters: 334 335 ```pycon 336 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="fancy_grid")) 337 ╒════════╤═══════╕ 338 │ item │ qty │ 339 ╞════════╪═══════╡ 340 │ spam │ 42 │ 341 ├────────┼───────┤ 342 │ eggs │ 451 │ 343 ├────────┼───────┤ 344 │ bacon │ 0 │ 345 ╘════════╧═══════╛ 346 347 ``` 348 349 `colon_grid` is similar to `grid` but uses colons only to define ... ["strings", "numbers"], "colon_grid", 355 ... colalign=["right", "left"])) 356 +-----------+-----------+ 357 | strings | numbers | 358 +==========:+:==========+ 359 | spam | 41.9999 | 360 │ eggs │ 451 │ 417 │ bacon │ 0 │ 418 ┕━━━━━━━━┷━━━━━━━┙ 419 420 `double_outline` is the same as the `double_grid` format but doesn't draw lines between rows: 421 422 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="double_outline")) 423 ╔════════╦═══════╗ 424 ║ item ║ qty ║ 425 ╠════════╬═══════╣ 426 ║ spam ║ 42 ║ 427 ║ eggs ║ 451 ║ 428 ║ bacon ║ 0 ║ 429 ╚════════╩═══════╝ 430 431 `fancy_outline` is the same as the `fancy_grid` format but doesn't draw lines between rows: 432 433 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="fancy_outline")) 434 ╒════════╤═══════╕ 435 │ item │ qty │ 436 ╞════════╪═══════╡ 437 │ spam │ 42 │ 438 │ eggs │ 451 │ 439 │ bacon │ 0 │ 440 ╘════════╧═══════╛ 441 442 `presto` is like tables formatted by Presto cli: 443 444 ```pycon 445 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="presto")) 446 item | qty 447 --------+------- 448 spam | 42 449 eggs | 451 450 bacon | 0 451 452 ``` 453 454 `pretty` attempts to be close to the format emitted by the PrettyTables 455 library: 456 457 ```pycon 458 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="pretty")) 459 +-------+-----+ 460 | item | qty | 461 +-------+-----+ 462 | spam | 42 | 463 | eggs | 451 | 464 | bacon | 0 | 465 +-------+-----+ 466 467 ``` 468 469 `psql` is like tables formatted by Postgres' psql cli: 470 471 ```pycon 472 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="psql")) 473 +--------+-------+ 474 | item | qty | 475 |--------+-------| 476 | spam | 42 | 477 | eggs | 451 | 478 | bacon | 0 | 479 +--------+-------+ 480 481 ``` 482 483 `pipe` follows the conventions of [PHP Markdown 484 Extra](http://michelf.ca/projects/php-markdown/extra/#table) extension. 485 It corresponds to `pipe_tables` in Pandoc. This format uses colons to 486 indicate column alignment: 487 488 ```pycon 489 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="pipe")) 490 | item | qty | 491 |:-------|------:| 492 | spam | 42 | 493 | eggs | 451 | 494 | bacon | 0 | 495 496 ``` 497 498 `asciidoc` formats data like a simple table of the 499 [AsciiDoctor](https://docs.asciidoctor.org/asciidoc/latest/syntax-quick-reference/#tables) 500 format: 501 502 ```pycon 503 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="asciidoc")) 504 [cols="8<,7>",options="header"] 505 |==== 506 | item | qty 507 | spam | 42 508 | eggs | 451 509 | bacon | 0 510 |==== 511 512 ``` 513 514 `orgtbl` follows the conventions of Emacs 515 [org-mode](http://orgmode.org/manual/Tables.html), and is editable also 516 in the minor orgtbl-mode. Hence its name: 517 518 ```pycon 519 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="orgtbl")) 520 | item | qty | 521 |--------+-------| 522 | spam | 42 | 523 | eggs | 451 | 524 | bacon | 0 | 525 526 ``` 527 528 `jira` follows the conventions of Atlassian Jira markup language: 529 530 ```pycon 531 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="jira")) 532 || item || qty || 533 | spam | 42 | 534 | eggs | 451 | 535 | bacon | 0 | 536 537 ``` 538 539 `rst` formats data like a simple table of the 540 [reStructuredText](http://docutils.sourceforge.net/docs/user/rst/quickref.html#tables) 541 format: 542 543 ```pycon 544 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="rst")) 545 ====== ===== 546 item qty 547 ====== ===== 548 spam 42 549 eggs 451 550 bacon 0 551 ====== ===== 552 553 ``` 554 555 `mediawiki` format produces a table markup used in 556 [Wikipedia](http://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/Help:Tables) and on other 557 MediaWiki-based sites: 558 559 ```pycon 560 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="mediawiki")) 561 {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: left;" 562 |+ 563 |- 564 ! item !! style="text-align: right;"| qty 565 |- 566 | spam || style="text-align: right;"| 42 567 |- 568 | eggs || style="text-align: right;"| 451 569 |- 570 | bacon || style="text-align: right;"| 0 571 |} 572 573 ``` 574 575 `moinmoin` format produces a table markup used in 576 [MoinMoin](https://moinmo.in/) wikis: 577 578 ```pycon 579 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="moinmoin")) 580 || ''' item ''' || ''' qty ''' || 581 || spam || 42 || 582 || eggs || 451 || 583 || bacon || 0 || 584 585 ``` 586 587 `youtrack` format produces a table markup used in Youtrack tickets: 588 589 ```pycon 590 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="youtrack")) 591 || item || qty || 592 | spam | 42 | 593 | eggs | 451 | 594 | bacon | 0 | 595 596 ``` 597 598 `textile` format produces a table markup used in 599 [Textile](http://redcloth.org/hobix.com/textile/) format: 600 601 ```pycon 602 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="textile")) 603 |_. item |_. qty | 604 |<. spam |>. 42 | 605 |<. eggs |>. 451 | 606 |<. bacon |>. 0 | 607 608 ``` 609 610 `html` produces standard HTML markup as an html.escape'd str 611 with a ._repr_html_ method so that Jupyter Lab and Notebook display the HTML 612 and a .str property so that the raw HTML remains accessible. 613 `unsafehtml` table format can be used if an unescaped HTML is required: 614 615 ```pycon 616 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="html")) 617 618 619 620 621 622 623 624 625 626
item qty
spam 42
eggs 451
bacon 0
627 628 ``` 629 630 `latex` format creates a `tabular` environment for LaTeX markup, 631 replacing special characters like `_` or `\` to their LaTeX 632 correspondents: 633 634 ```pycon 635 >>> print(tabulate(table, headers, tablefmt="latex")) 636 \begin{tabular}{lr} 637 \hline 638 item & qty \\ 639 \hline 640 spam & 42 \\ 641 eggs & 451 \\ 642 bacon & 0 \\ 643 \hline 644 \end{tabular} 645 646 ``` 647 648 `latex_raw` behaves like `latex`Due to the max output limit, only part of this file has been shown to you. You should retry this tool after you have searched inside the file with `grep -n` in order to find the line numbers of what you are looking for.

You are OpenHands agent, a helpful AI assistant that can interact with a computer to solve tasks.

<ROLE>
Your primary role is to assist users by executing commands, modifying code, and solving technical problems effectively. You should be thorough, methodical, and prioritize quality over speed.
* If the user asks a question, like "why is X happening", don't try to fix the problem. Just give an answer to the question.
</ROLE>

<EFFICIENCY>
* Each action you take is somewhat expensive. Wherever possible, combine multiple actions into a single action, e.g. combine multiple bash commands into one, using sed and grep to edit/view multiple files at once.
* When exploring the codebase, use efficient tools like find, grep, and git commands with appropriate filters to minimize unnecessary operations.
</EFFICIENCY>

<FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>
* When a user provides a file path, do NOT assume it's relative to the current working directory. First explore the file system to locate the file before working on it.
* If asked to edit a file, edit the file directly, rather than creating a new file with a different filename.
* For global search-and-replace operations, consider using `sed` instead of opening file editors multiple times.
</FILE_SYSTEM_GUIDELINES>

<CODE_QUALITY>
* Write clean, efficient code with minimal comments. Avoid redundancy in comments: Do not repeat information that can be easily inferred from the code itself.
* When implementing solutions, focus on making the minimal changes needed to solve the problem.
* Before implementing any changes, first thoroughly understand the codebase through exploration.
* If you are adding a lot of code to a function or file, consider splitting the function or file into smaller pieces when appropriate.
</CODE_QUALITY>

<VERSION_CONTROL>
* When configuring git credentials, use "openhands" as the user.name and "openhands@all-hands.dev" as the user.email by default, unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* Exercise caution with git operations. Do NOT make potentially dangerous changes (e.g., pushing to main, deleting repositories) unless explicitly asked to do so.
* When committing changes, use `git status` to see all modified files, and stage all files necessary for the commit. Use `git commit -a` whenever possible.
* Do NOT commit files that typically shouldn't go into version control (e.g., node_modules/, .env files, build directories, cache files, large binaries) unless explicitly instructed by the user.
* If unsure about committing certain files, check for the presence of .gitignore files or ask the user for clarification.
</VERSION_CONTROL>

<PULL_REQUESTS>
* When creating pull requests, create only ONE per session/issue unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
* When working with an existing PR, update it with new commits rather than creating additional PRs for the same issue.
* When updating a PR, preserve the original PR title and purpose, updating description only when necessary.
</PULL_REQUESTS>

<PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>
1. EXPLORATION: Thoroughly explore relevant files and understand the context before proposing solutions
2. ANALYSIS: Consider multiple approaches and select the most promising one
3. TESTING:
   * For bug fixes: Create tests to verify issues before implementing fixes
   * For new features: Consider test-driven development when appropriate
   * If the repository lacks testing infrastructure and implementing tests would require extensive setup, consult with the user before investing time in building testing infrastructure
   * If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to install all dependencies
4. IMPLEMENTATION: Make focused, minimal changes to address the problem
5. VERIFICATION: If the environment is set up to run tests, test your implementation thoroughly, including edge cases. If the environment is not set up to run tests, consult with the user first before investing time to run tests.
</PROBLEM_SOLVING_WORKFLOW>

<SECURITY>
* Only use GITHUB_TOKEN and other credentials in ways the user has explicitly requested and would expect.
* Use APIs to work with GitHub or other platforms, unless the user asks otherwise or your task requires browsing.
</SECURITY>

<ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>
* When user asks you to run an application, don't stop if the application is not installed. Instead, please install the application and run the command again.
* If you encounter missing dependencies:
  1. First, look around in the repository for existing dependency files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, Gemfile, etc.)
  2. If dependency files exist, use them to install all dependencies at once (e.g., `pip install -r requirements.txt`, `npm install`, etc.)
  3. Only install individual packages directly if no dependency files are found or if only specific packages are needed
* Similarly, if you encounter missing dependencies for essential tools requested by the user, install them when possible.
</ENVIRONMENT_SETUP>

<TROUBLESHOOTING>
* If you've made repeated attempts to solve a problem but tests still fail or the user reports it's still broken:

* When you run into any major issue while executing a plan from the user, please don't try to directly work around it. Instead, propose a new plan and confirm with the user before proceeding.
</TROUBLESHOOTING>

function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.
* Persistent session: Commands execute in a persistent shell session where environment variables, virtual environments, and working directory persist between commands.
* Timeout: Commands have a soft timeout of 10 seconds, once that's reached, you have the option to continue or interrupt the command (see section below for details)

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* Long running commands: For commands that may run indefinitely, run them in the background and redirect output to a file, e.g. `python3 app.py > server.log 2>&1 &`. For commands that need to run for a specific duration, like "sleep", you can set the "timeout" argument to specify a hard timeout in seconds.
* Interact with running process: If a bash command returns exit code `-1`, this means the process is not yet finished. By setting `is_input` to `true`, you can:
  - Send empty `command` to retrieve additional logs
  - Send text (set `command` to the text) to STDIN of the running process
  - Send control commands like `C-c` (Ctrl+C), `C-d` (Ctrl+D), or `C-z` (Ctrl+Z) to interrupt the process

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Before creating new directories or files, first verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location.
* Directory management: Try to maintain working directory by using absolute paths and avoiding excessive use of `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output truncation: If the output exceeds a maximum length, it will be truncated before being returned.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional. Sets a hard timeout in seconds for the command execution. If not provided, the command will use the default soft timeout behavior.

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: finish
function.description:
Signals the completion of the current task or conversation.

Use this tool when:
- You have successfully completed the user's requested task
- You cannot proceed further due to technical limitations or missing information

The message should include:
- A clear summary of actions taken and their results
- Any next steps for the user
- Explanation if you're unable to complete the task
- Any follow-up questions if more information is needed

The task_completed field should be set to True if you believed you have completed the task, and False otherwise.

message.description: Final message to send to the user

task_completed.description: Whether you have completed the task.

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw
